item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The reference is completely intentional! I need to get back into Worm again— I stopped reading shortly after Leviathan attacked Brockton Bay, but it's a really great series.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1+

This is a great UIU murder mystery. A fantastic use of data redaction that makes sense. Both in-universe and to make it spooky. Loved all the logs.

Anyway, there's a question I just have to ask. It's about a detail pointed out to me by a poster on another site.

The nickname of the killer is the "Brockton Bay Binder". Together with the main focus of the article being creepy crawly spiders, is that an intentional reference to the Worm series of web novels? The main character's base of operations is in a city of the same name, and she has the power to control spiders and similar creatures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Christ this is good. I found myself desperately trying to figure out what the hell the killer was doing to those bodies - you do a great job of giving just enough detail to stimulate the imagination without giving anything resembling a full picture. The two things that fell flat was that the body at the end was made of spiders (felt over the top and too different from the smaller but still disturbing changes the meme makes), and that the meme randomly started affecting women. Upvoted regardless, this is one of the best examples of expungement on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Commenting here to bring attention to a dead image.

Er, there it is. Probably a wikidot issue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this. I have already given it a +1. I do have a bit of a nitpick, though.

The percentages given for the common criteria between susceptible people don't pan out.

If there have been 39 observed instances:

92% would be 35.88 people
90% would be 35.1 people
89% would be 34.71 people
54% would be 21.06 people

You could just give numbers of each after giving the 39 total at the beginning, give fractions (or "X out of 39") or do non-integer percentages (tenths should be sufficient to be more accurate).
It's not a huge deal, but it did bug me a bit.

Potential replacement values that are close to the originals:

36/39 (92.3%)
35/39 (89.7%)
34/39 (87.2%)
21/39 (53.8%)

item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. The author may revert at any time.

+ show block
– hide block

Alright. Might be cheating again, but reached out to Ihp with respect to this one, as it left me quite stumped. In addition to a link to the UIU Hub, the reference to MI666 can be linked out their first appearance in Operation LLEWYN DARK. Finally, Ryan Melbourne is mentioned via the Euler-Melbourne test and is a character from Friday. As such, I am suggesting the following:

Editing this:

When I was at Quantico, one of the things that was made clear to us: the FBI, not just Unusual Incidents, is a bunch of fuckups. Mulder and Scully aren't absurd because they hunt bigfoot and aliens; they're absurd because they get shit done. It's 80% paperwork, maybe more; one of my first assignments in training was to write an archival request so I could access a gallery of shit that's been sent to Manson over the years. I… failed that.

To this:

When I was at Quantico, one of the things that was made clear to us: the FBI, not just Unusual Incidents, is a bunch of fuckups. Mulder and Scully aren't absurd because they hunt bigfoot and aliens; they're absurd because they get shit done. It's 80% paperwork, maybe more; one of my first assignments in training was to write an archival request so I could access a gallery of shit that's been sent to Manson over the years. I… failed that.

Editing this:

Colloquialism referring to the Unusual Incidents Unit's British equivalent, The British Occult Service, or MI666.

To this:

Colloquialism referring to the Unusual Incidents Unit's British equivalent, The British Occult Service, or MI666.

Editing this:

Individuals who are part of Task Force WEBCUTTER are to be given monthly physical and psychological exams. In the event that any agent shows signs of being affected by SCP-3256, they are to be immediately contained and sent to Site-92 for treatment. New personnel joining WEBCUTTER are to be given a standard Euler-Melbourne Memetic Hazard Resistance Test (Or simply Euler-Melbourne Test); individuals who score lower than a threshold of 74 are to be reassigned.

To this:

Individuals who are part of Task Force WEBCUTTER are to be given monthly physical and psychological exams. In the event that any agent shows signs of being affected by SCP-3256, they are to be immediately contained and sent to Site-92 for treatment. New personnel joining WEBCUTTER are to be given a standard Euler-Melbourne Memetic Hazard Resistance Test (Or simply Euler-Melbourne Test); individuals who score lower than a threshold of 74 are to be reassigned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 'Everything is spiders' shall be the whole of the law.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In greek myth there was a woman named Arachne (a mortal) that challenged Athena to a weaving competition. It's inconsistent as to who won, but in either case, after the competition, Athena turned Arachne into a spider (the first spider, in fact). Presumably, that is the reference. And yes, this is why spiders are "arachnids."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(Kingsmith begins picking at their nose as the phrase 'Athena's loom is shattered' is spoken.)

Boston: Okay, that got a reaction. Let's see, 'Athena's loom' corresponds to— oh shit.

What's the significance of the phrase? Is it a reference to something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, I don't usually freak at spider-induced horror.

I'm kinda freaking. Take your upvote, you monster.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ahhh! Hot holy christ on a trampoline, I loved every second of this!

This is a slow-burn procedural done so, so right; I was on the edge of my seat start-to-finish. You get triple bonus points for some of the best expungement I've seen in some time - skirting neatly around multiple highly insidious details in such a way as to never quite shine a light on the unfolding horror, but it never feels lazy or unimaginative.

+1, Imma plug the fuck out of this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Call the UIU UIuseless all you want, but they are fun to read about.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If I can say anything about this, it's that it never felt as long as it is. I kept looking at the tiny, shrinking scrollbar and going, holy shit, I've been reading this for a while, haven't I?

Of course, now I'm despondent, since I've been working on an article whose vector is through crime TV shows too, so fuck me, I guess. D:

I fixed one typo, but there are more. Is it supposed to be Adler or Alder?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a lot here that feels like it could be edited or formatted differently; however, these are mostly issues of presentation rather than content (I feel very strongly that an article should look nice and clean, for example).

Despite that, there's a lot here that I like, and I actually found myself wanting to read more and more as the article went on. It's a nice, slowly paced police procedural story that — while the conclusion is somewhat predictable — still managed to make me want to see it through to the end. There's also a lot of nice little lore-building touches throughout (the smiler, for example).

I think this article could use significant amounts of tightening, but what's here is meaty and good enough that this never actually interfered with me enjoying it. So, solid +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That was some solid use of redactions and expungements for creep factor. Even though I started figuring out it was spider related it was still unsettling as hell. The start did feel slow, but the more I read the more engrossed I got.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It started in slow, then it started to grow.

This began disturbing and went asymptotic by the end. I do like a good bit of body horror, and the way the details were gradually revealed was very effective.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It seems this Skip is trying to turn people into angry spiders. I think that's your favorite kind of thing, is it not? :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is bad. Not bad is in a bad article, but bad as in something that is evil. Needless to say, i love this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great use of redacted information to make something more spooky. I had to stop reading a few times as I am really not a fan of spiders.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is goddamn stellar writing, ihp. It's one of the few SCPs I can confidently say shows that the author loves what they're writing about, and I thought I noticed a bit/some/a lot of Mindhunter's influence in there.

Congrats, you got me to read and enjoy a super-long skip. Now I feel dirty. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You got me freaked out by spiders. That doesn't sound special, but keep in mind, I love spiders. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loved this as a draft, love it here. +1

item.title: This is what happens when you marathon Criminal Minds and Mindhunter.
item.content:encoded:
Before anyone asks: no, this was not originally going to be an entry for the Murder Mystery contest, though I wish I could have put that in, but it only accepted new work, and this had been on my sandbox for yonks— since before I wrote Black Autumn, even.

As the title of this post suggests, this is the result of me marathoning several TV shows of varying quality, form trashy procedural shows like Criminal Minds to uncomfortable masterpieces like Mindhunter to the excellent Fringe.

My thought process was: "Hey, we don't have a serial killer as a skip" and then the murder mystery contest happened and now we have about ten. :T

Any errors are unintentional. If you see any glaring ones, please drive to my house and punch me in the face with corrections (by "drive to my house" I mean "post here" and "punch me in the face" I mean "punch me in the face".)

Thanks to my beta readers: DarkStuff, DrChandra, Presque, Tanhony, MayD, and anyone else who I've missed over the last several months of me getting this picked over.

Image Attribution: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Crime_scene#/media/File:FBI_Evidence_Response_Team_2.jpg

In the public domain, originally taken from FBI.gov, found on Wikipedia.
